Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine. I would tell myself I'd study on certain days, but it never stuck. Most of the time I'd just wait until something felt urgent enough to deal with, and even then I'd only do the bare minimum. My weeks were pretty chaotic and studying always ended up at the bottom of my priorities.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and try to cram everything in at once. It wasn't really studying so much as panic-mode scrambling. Regular study times just never happened for me this semester.
Q3: Usually it was because I got a notification about something due, or I'd feel a wave of guilt about how much I was falling behind. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything critical. It was rarely because I actually wanted to engage with the material—more like damage control.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, but it never stuck. Life kept getting in the way, and honestly the platform just felt like another chore. I never reached a point where logging in felt natural or automatic.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful when I actually watched them, because I learn better when I can see things being demonstrated rather than just reading. But even then, I'd often zone out halfway through. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful to see how other people understood things, but I rarely participated myself.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and the optional practice exercises. Reading on a screen is hard for me—I need to do things with my hands to really learn. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. They just felt like extra work I couldn't handle on top of everything else.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly tied to my grade and I could see immediate results from doing it. If something felt abstract or disconnected from what I'd be tested on, I just couldn't bring myself to care. I needed to feel like my effort would actually count for something tangible.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. If it wasn't required, my brain automatically categorized it as 'not my problem.' Required activities at least had consequences attached, so I'd force myself through them even when I didn't want to. The optional stuff just felt like extra weight I couldn't carry.
Q9: Yeah, sometimes I'd download lecture slides or PDFs and look at them on my phone when I was on the bus or waiting somewhere. I also tried watching some YouTube videos on the same topics because sometimes outside explanations clicked better for me. None of that would show up in the system though.
Q10: There were times I'd print out notes and spread them on my bed trying to make sense of things, or I'd be talking through concepts with my roommate who's in a different program. I'd also sometimes just sit and think about problems while doing other things, like working. That kind of mental processing doesn't leave any digital trace.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a video or document once and then never go back to the LMS for it. I'd have it saved on my laptop and reference it offline when I needed to. It was easier than dealing with the platform every time, especially when my internet was being slow or unreliable.
Q12: Things got really hard around midterms when I was also picking up extra shifts at work because I needed the money. I was exhausted all the time and just felt this heavy sadness that made everything seem pointless. The course material started feeling impossible to catch up on, so I kind of just... stopped trying as hard.
Q13: Absolutely. I was constantly tired from working, and when I did have time to study, I was too mentally drained to focus. The confusion built up because I'd missed so much, and then the workload felt insurmountable. I'd open the LMS, feel overwhelmed by how behind I was, and just close it again.
Q14: So many times. I'd log in, click through some pages, maybe start a video, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about bills, or feeling anxious about other things, and the content just wouldn't sink in. It was like going through the motions without actually being present.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was avoidance—the anxiety of starting something I didn't understand felt worse than the anxiety of not doing it. I'd tell myself I'd feel more ready tomorrow, but tomorrow I'd feel the same way. It became a vicious cycle.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. Without them, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all. But I'd always wait until the last possible moment, which meant my work was rushed and not my best. The deadline pressure was stressful but also the only structure I had.
Q17: When I did get feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly, but mostly I felt too far behind for it to matter. If the feedback was critical, it just made me feel worse about myself and less likely to try again. I needed encouragement but also felt like I didn't deserve it given how little effort I was putting in.
Q18: The course structure felt really rigid and didn't work well for how I learn. Everything was online and self-paced in theory, but there were still these strict deadlines that I couldn't keep up with. I needed more hands-on activities and maybe more check-ins to keep me accountable, but instead I just drifted.
Q19: The lack of interactive or practical elements made it harder for me. I'm a kinesthetic learner—I need to do things, not just read or watch. Also, the way everything was laid out in the LMS was confusing sometimes, and I'd waste time just trying to find what I needed. That frustration added up.
Q20: I think sometimes you're trying to figure things out, clicking around, rewatching videos because nothing makes sense the first time. Or maybe you're anxious and checking the platform obsessively without actually completing anything. High activity doesn't mean you're making progress—sometimes it means you're lost or struggling.
Q21: I guess some people are just more efficient, or they study in ways that don't involve the LMS much. Maybe they have better resources outside of class, or they learn well from textbooks and don't need all the online stuff. For me, low activity usually meant I was disengaged, but I can see how it could be different for others.
Q22: The log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were just going through the motions. It misses all the offline studying, the mental processing, the conversations with classmates. It also can't capture how you were feeling—whether you were engaged or just present.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's wanting to understand and being mentally present. For me, I wasn't really engaged this semester. I was just surviving, trying to get through without completely failing.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how sad and overwhelmed I felt most of the time, or how much I was struggling financially and emotionally. It wouldn't capture the guilt I felt every time I avoided studying, or the way my motivation just disappeared when things got hard. The data shows what I did, but not why I couldn't do more.
